strengths: Introduces a latent diffusion model over short motion primitives for autoregressive, online text-driven generation, enabling real-time (300+ fps) long-horizon synthesis with only 10 diffusion steps.

Unifies semantic (text) and spatial control by operating in the diffusion noise space: both gradient-based latent noise optimization and RL control policies over latent actions are demonstrated within the same model.
Shows strong empirical performance: superior FID and transition smoothness to state-of-the-art FlowMDM with 10x speed and much lower memory, plus human preference wins on realism and semantics.
Scheduled training with rollout histories materially improves long-term stability and text controllability, and the primitive VAE effectively suppresses motion artifacts versus raw-space diffusion.

Versatile applications: online temporal composition, text-conditioned in-betweening (quantitative gains over DNO/OmniControl), goal-reaching via RL with multiple locomotion styles, and qualitative human-scene interactions.
weaknesses: Reliance on fine-grained, frame-aligned text labels (BABEL) limits generalization; performance degrades on coarse caption datasets (HML3D) and open-vocabulary prompts.

Scene-conditioned control is primarily qualitative with limited quantitative evaluation of contact/collision beyond simple constraints; broader comparisons and metrics for human-scene interactions are missing.
Kinematic generation can exhibit physics artifacts (e.g., skating), and while a physics-tracking post-process is shown, it is not integrated into core evaluations.
Some presentation issues (minor equation/notation glitches) may hinder full reproducibility, and RL experiments are confined to simple flat-floor settings with limited baselines.
reviewers.summary_comment: This paper presents DART, a diffusion-based autoregressive motion primitive model that delivers real-time, online text-driven motion generation and precise spatial control by operating in a compact latent space. The combination of short motion primitives, a latent VAE, scheduled training, and diffusion sampling yields fast, stable rollouts and strong performance on temporal composition, with human studies favoring the method over prior art. The latent-space control formulation is notably versatile, supporting both gradient-based optimization (for in-betweening and scene-aware goals) and RL policies (for text-conditioned goal reaching) using the same generative backbone. While the approach depends on fine-grained labeled data, has limited quantitative evaluation for complex human-scene interactions, and may present kinematic artifacts, the core idea is creative, impactful, and well-supported by experiments. Given the novelty and potential to push real-time, language-driven motion control forward, I recommend acceptance.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: The work is ML-centric (latent diffusion, VAE representation learning, autoregressive modeling, scheduled training) with connections to RL control, aligning with ICML topics in deep learning, probabilistic methods, optimization, RL, and application-driven ML in vision/robotics.

Contribution fit: Proposes an ML method and training scheme (motion-primitive latent diffusion with scheduled training), plus a unified latent-space control interface supporting both gradient-based optimization and RL policies—contributions ICML recognizes as algorithmic and systems-oriented.

Rigor fit: Strong empirical evaluation with quantitative metrics, human preference studies, ablations (VAE vs raw space, scheduled training, diffusion steps), and detailed speed/memory profiling; this level of rigor meets ICML standards.

Novelty fit: While many components (diffusion, VAE, RL) are known, their integration around short motion primitives for real-time online text control and spatial control is a fresh package; reviewer notes novelty/significance as solid (4/5).
Presentation fit: Generally clear and well-motivated, with extensive experimental sections; reviewer cites clarity as strong overall (4/5).
why_it_does_not_fit: Application focus: The paper is squarely in computer vision/graphics (human motion synthesis/control). ICML often favors broader ML theory/algorithms over domain-specific systems; limited theoretical insights may reduce fit.
Reviewer-raised generalization limits: Dependence on fine-grained BABEL labels and degraded performance on coarse/open-vocab prompts may be viewed as data-specific and limit general ML impact.

Rigor gaps in certain tasks: Scene-conditioned control is primarily qualitative with limited quantitative evaluation; physics artifacts (skating) remain, and RL is demonstrated in simplified flat-floor settings with limited baselines—potentially below ICML expectations for breadth/depth across settings.
Minor presentation glitches: Reviewer notes notation/equation issues that could hinder reproducibility—ICML reviewing can be strict about clarity.

strengths: The authors will add concrete, quantitative human-scene interaction metrics (collision rate, penetration depth, foot contact consistency, goal accuracy, skating) and release scenes and evaluation code, directly addressing a prior evaluation gap with reproducible measures.

They commit to new experiments demonstrating weaker-supervision viability: a pseudo-alignment pipeline on HML3D with reported R-prec/MM-Dist/FID/jerk, plus an ablation of hierarchical conditioning versus flat conditioning, strengthening claims about generalization beyond frame-aligned labels.

They will quantify optimization accelerations via ablations on wall-clock time, iterations, and quality (goal error, skating, jerk) for in-betweening and scene-aware tasks, and document asynchronous/MPC setups and policy distillation to bridge optimization and real-time control.
Technical clarity is improved via a corrected denoiser parameterization (x0-prediction) and standardized notation/symbols across equations and algorithms, aiding reproducibility.

Core contributions remain strong: a latent diffusion model over motion primitives enabling real-time online text-driven generation, and unified spatial control via latent-noise optimization and RL without paired control–motion data.
weaknesses: The added HML3D pseudo-alignment and hierarchical conditioning experiments, as well as optimization speedup ablations, are planned for the camera-ready and not yet part of the current submission; thus, evidence for these extensions is pending.

While the pseudo-alignment relies on retrieval trained on frame-aligned data, broader open-vocabulary generalization remains future work and depends on additional supervision (e.g., video-derived labels or VLM descriptors).
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors’ follow-up effectively strengthens the submission. They will provide quantitative human-scene interaction metrics and code, add empirical validation of weaker-supervision strategies on HML3D (pseudo-alignment and hierarchical conditioning), and quantify the impact of proposed optimization accelerations, alongside documenting asynchronous setups and distillation. They have corrected the denoiser parameterization and standardized notation, improving clarity. Although these additions are slated for the camera-ready and not yet reflected in the current version, the core paper already presents a novel and impactful framework—latent diffusion over motion primitives for real-time, online text-driven control with versatile spatial control via optimization and RL—and strong empirical performance. With the planned additions, the evaluation and practical applicability will be further reinforced. I maintain an accept recommendation.

strengths: The rebuttal thoroughly clarifies the in-betweening evaluation: explicit endpoint anchoring for all methods, dual reporting (anchored/unanchored) for baselines, exclusion of anchored frames from jerk/skate, and the introduction of a start continuity error metric with a promise to release the evaluation scripts and seeds.

Spatial control evaluation will be substantially strengthened with quantitative human-scene interaction metrics (collision rate, penetration depth, contact consistency, foot–floor distance, goal accuracy) on shared scenes and a DNO baseline under identical SDF constraints, alongside released scenes/SDFs and code.

Clarity and reproducibility are improved: denoiser parameterization and notation are corrected; exact repositories, checkpoints, and preprocessing for FID/R-prec/MM-Dist will be enumerated, and evaluation harnesses will be released.

Reinforcement-learning evaluation will better isolate controller effectiveness via a walk-only comparison against GAMMA on shared waypoint tasks and an optimization-versus-RL ablation, with standardized metrics and seeds.

Methodological core remains compelling: a latent diffusion model over motion primitives with scheduled training achieves real-time performance with only 10 diffusion steps and provides a unified latent space for both gradient-based control and RL policies.
weaknesses: Several critical improvements are promised for the camera-ready but are not yet reflected in the current draft (quantitative HSI metrics/baselines, clarified in-betweening protocol with start continuity reporting, and expanded RL comparisons). The strength of the final conclusions depends on successful incorporation of these additions.

Although a pseudo-alignment pipeline for coarse labels is proposed, the approach still fundamentally relies on fine-grained frame-aligned labels for best text–motion alignment; generalization to open-vocabulary and weak supervision remains limited and is positioned as future work.

Optimization-based control remains computationally expensive for longer sequences (noted runtime), and sensitivity analyses (e.g., guidance scale, sampler variants) are not yet reported; while RL mitigates runtime, further characterization would strengthen practical guidance.
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors have satisfactorily addressed the principal concerns about evaluation fairness, spatial control quantification, and clarity. They provide precise protocol updates for in-betweening (endpoint anchoring, start continuity error, anchored/unanchored reporting) with a commitment to release scripts; they will add quantitative human-scene interaction metrics and a DNO baseline on shared scenes with released SDFs; they have corrected denoiser notation and will document evaluation encoders and repositories; and they will strengthen RL analysis with a walk-only comparison against GAMMA and an optimization-versus-RL ablation. These additions, coupled with the already strong results in temporal composition, real-time performance, and versatile control mechanisms, make the paper solid in soundness and evaluation. While some elements are pending integration and the reliance on fine-grained labels persists, the planned updates are specific and actionable. I recommend acceptance, expecting the promised quantitative additions and reproducibility materials to be included in the camera-ready.

strengths: Addresses a practical and impactful need: real-time, online text-driven motion composition with strong efficiency (Table 1 reports ~334 fps, 0.02 s latency, ~2.3 GiB vs. FlowMDM’s ~31 fps, 161 s latency, ~11.9 GiB).
Autoregressive motion-primitive design with a latent VAE+diffusion and scheduled training achieves stable long rollouts with only 10 diffusion steps, reducing jitter and enabling responsive control.

Unifies semantic and spatial control via latent-noise optimization and RL in diffusion-noise space; delivers strong in-betweening (Table 3: 0.59 cm goal error vs. 4.24 cm for DNO) and text-conditioned goal-reaching (Table 4: 100% success with lower skate/floor distance than GAMMA).
Evaluation is multi-faceted (standard quantitative metrics, human preference in Table 2, and system profiling) and convincingly demonstrates both motion quality and practical deployability.

Author rebuttal constructively addresses remaining concerns with concrete additions: quantitative HSI metrics/baselines and scene releases, RL extensions to non-flat terrains, optimization accelerations (windowed optimization, low-dim noise bases, DDIM inversion, coarse-to-fine, async/MPC), label-efficient training (pseudo-alignment, hierarchical conditioning), and clarified notation plus time-lag–aware semantic analysis.
weaknesses: Best alignment still depends on frame-level labels; while pseudo-alignment and hierarchical conditioning are planned, open-vocabulary and coarse-caption settings remain weaker and the new results are not yet in the main paper.
Current draft lacks quantitative human–scene interaction benchmarks and broader RL tests beyond flat, 2D goals; the promised additions are valuable but remain to be integrated.
Optimization-based control can be slow for longer sequences (~74 s for 60 frames as reported), which may limit interactivity unless using the proposed accelerations or RL controllers.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper introduces DART, a diffusion-based autoregressive motion-primitive model that achieves real-time, text-driven motion generation and supports precise spatial control through latent-noise optimization and RL. The approach is practically compelling and well-supported: it attains strong motion quality with very few diffusion steps, composes long sequences online, and substantially improves efficiency over offline baselines (e.g., Table 1). It is preferred by human raters (Table 2) and outperforms relevant baselines on in-betweening (Table 3) and goal-reaching (Table 4). My main reservations were reliance on frame-aligned labels, the lack of quantitative HSI evaluation and more challenging control settings, and the cost of optimization-based control. The rebuttal addresses these with concrete commitments—adding HSI metrics and baselines with released scenes/SDFs, extending RL to non-flat terrains, providing acceleration strategies with ablations, and introducing label-efficient training variants—along with notation fixes and a time-lag–aware semantic analysis. While some additions are not yet reflected in the current draft, the core contribution is significant, the methodology is sound, and the clarity and practical relevance are strong. I recommend acceptance.

why_it_fits: Application-driven ML contribution at the CV/graphics–ML boundary: a latent diffusion model over motion primitives for real-time, online text-driven motion generation plus unified spatial control via latent-noise optimization and RL, which aligns with ICML’s application-driven ML and RL tracks.

Strong empirical focus and systems aspects (10 diffusion steps, ~334 fps, low latency/memory) match ICML interest in ML systems and efficiency; reviewers praised soundness and significance (scores ~4–5) and the authors commit to releasing code, metrics, and evaluation harnesses.

strengths: Introduces a simple, architecture-agnostic per-layer rescaling scheme (SWR) that leverages proportionality to control weight growth with negligible computational overhead.
Provides theoretical insights: proves proportionality under homogeneous activations, establishes boundedness of weight norms under mild assumptions, and shows a tendency to improve layer balance.
Demonstrates consistent empirical gains across warm-start, continual learning (full and limited access), and standard supervised settings, with notably strong improvements on VGG-16/TinyImageNet.
Method preserves previously learned information compared to re-initialization approaches, mitigating plasticity loss without catastrophic resets.
Practical considerations for networks with normalization layers and an explicit algorithm make the method easy to adopt.
weaknesses: Theoretical guarantees rely on strong assumptions (homogeneous activations, bounded per-step norm changes) and the treatment of normalization layers is not rigorously justified; batch norm’s training-time behavior may violate proportionality.

Experimental comparison set is limited: misses stronger or closely related baselines (e.g., spectral norm regularization, path-SGD, recent normalization-aware techniques) and more diverse architectures (e.g., ResNets, Transformers).
Sensitivity to learning rate schedules requires an extra heuristic (SWR + re-init), suggesting potential fragility and adding complexity to practical use.
reviewers.summary_comment: This paper proposes Soft Weight Rescaling (SWR), a lightweight per-step, per-layer rescaling scheme that softly steers weight norms toward their initialization while preserving proportionality, thereby addressing plasticity loss due to unbounded weight growth. The idea is refreshingly simple yet conceptually appealing, with theoretical arguments that bound weight norms and promote inter-layer balance. Empirically, SWR improves warm-start and continual learning performance and also yields notable gains in standard generalization, particularly on VGG-16/TinyImageNet. While some theoretical assumptions are idealized—especially regarding normalization layers—and the evaluation lacks comparisons to some stronger baselines and architectures, the core idea is novel enough and shows promising impact with minimal overhead. On balance, the paper advances the discussion on weight growth and plasticity with a practical, easy-to-use method, warranting acceptance.

Contribution type: Method + theory + empirical evaluation aligns with ICML’s preference for principled training methods; the paper offers a lightweight, architecture-agnostic algorithm with theoretical bounds on weight norms and layer balance.

Practical impact: Reviewer notes consistent gains across warm-start, continual (full/limited access), and standard training, with notable improvements on VGG-16/TinyImageNet—indicative of broad applicability valued at ICML.
why_it_does_not_fit: Rigor concerns: Reviewer flags strong/idealized assumptions (homogeneous activations, bounded per-step norm changes) and incomplete treatment of normalization layers; ICML often expects tighter theoretical grounding for widely applicable training methods.

Evaluation breadth: Missing stronger baselines (e.g., spectral norm regularization, path-SGD) and more diverse architectures (ResNets, Transformers) per reviewer—ICML typically expects thorough comparisons and diversity of models.

Practical fragility: Sensitivity to learning-rate schedules requiring SWR + re-init heuristic (as noted by the reviewer) may be viewed as added complexity that undercuts methodological robustness at ICML.

strengths: Soft Weight Rescaling (SWR) is a simple, per-step, layerwise rescaling that preserves functional proportionality via principled bias scaling (Sec. 3.2–3.3, Algorithm 1), directly targeting weight growth and plasticity without discarding learned information.

Theory plus practice: proportionality construction (Theorem 1), boundedness under mild conditions, and improved balancedness are coherent; the rebuttal strengthens soundness by clarifying the bounded per-step norm-change assumption and committing to a formal proposition for normalization layers, including running-statistics handling.

Empirical gains are meaningful in warm-start and continual learning (Figs. 2–4), and in standard training, notably improving VGG-16 on TinyImageNet (Table 1: from about 0.391 to 0.435). With learning-rate decay, SWR also shows large gains on VGG-16 (Table 5: to about 0.522).

The authors commit to substantially broaden baselines (spectral norm, Path-SGD/weight balancing, interval-based rescaling, AdamW, weight clipping) and to evaluate modern architectures (ResNet, Wide-ResNet, small ViT/DeiT) under the same warm-start, continual, and single-task protocols with reported overhead.

Practical robustness is being addressed: beyond the heuristic SWR+re-init, the authors will add adaptive variants (LR-coupled λ and EMA targets), provide sensitivity analyses for λ_f/λ_c, report per-step overhead, and release reproducible code and configurations.
weaknesses: Several key enhancements are promised rather than present: broader baselines and modern architectures are not yet in the manuscript, limiting the current empirical breadth and making the evaluation score remain conservative.

Soundness and usability around normalization layers and learning-rate schedules still depend on forthcoming additions (block-wise formalization with BN running statistics, adaptive variants). Current results rely on fixed λ with a re-init heuristic and separate λ_f and λ_c, which can require nontrivial tuning; in Table 1, L2 outperforms SWR on CNN-BN, suggesting unresolved failure modes.

Algorithm 1 and proportionality with normalization would benefit from additional precision (e.g., explicit definition of k, accumulation of C across blocks, behavior for layers without biases), which the authors plan to add but is not yet reflected.
reviewers.summary_comment: This work proposes a clean, low-overhead mechanism—Soft Weight Rescaling—that bounds weight growth while preserving proportionality, thereby improving plasticity without re-initialization. The idea is novel relative to common penalties and reset strategies, theoretically motivated (proportional networks; boundedness; balancedness), and empirically promising: SWR narrows the warm-start gap and improves continual learning (Figs. 2–4), and yields notable boosts on larger models (e.g., VGG-16 on TinyImageNet improves from roughly 0.391 to 0.435; with LR decay to about 0.522). The rebuttal credibly addresses prior concerns by committing to stronger baselines (spectral norm, Path-SGD, prior rescaling, AdamW, clipping), modern architectures (ResNet, Wide-ResNet, small ViT/DeiT), a formal treatment of normalization layers (including running statistics), and adaptive variants that mitigate LR-schedule sensitivity, along with sensitivity analyses and overhead reporting. While these additions are not yet in the manuscript—so I keep soundness and evaluation at acceptable levels—the core idea and potential impact are strong and align with the conference’s emphasis on forward-looking contributions. I recommend acceptance, contingent on incorporating the promised expansions and clarifications in the final version.

strengths: SWR remains a simple, low-overhead procedure that empirically improves performance across warm-start, continual learning, and standard training; e.g., Table 1 reports a VGG-16/TinyImageNet gain from 0.3912 ± 0.0142 to 0.4348 ± 0.0025.

The rebuttal provides a formal block-wise proportionality proposition for BN/LN, detailing scaling of γ, β and optional rescaling of running statistics, which, if integrated, would address the prior ad hoc treatment.

Clear plan to revise Algorithm 1 with block segmentation around normalization layers and explicit accumulation of per-block scalers, which should resolve ambiguity about how C is accumulated when k marks the last normalization.

Strengthening of theoretical assumptions is proposed via a lemma with sufficient conditions (bounded gradients/step sizes or clipping) and empirical validation (histograms of per-step norm changes), and moving the loose Lipschitz corollary to the appendix.

Substantial expansion of baselines and architectures is promised (AdamW, spectral norm, Path/weight-balancing, interval rescaling; ResNet/WideResNet and a small ViT), alongside improved fairness (matched hyperparameter budgets, full S&P), increased seeds, and code release.
weaknesses: Core fixes are still prospective: the BN/LN proportionality proof, running-statistics treatment, and revised block-wise algorithm are not yet present in the manuscript; Algorithm 1 remains ambiguous about cumulative scaling with normalization (e.g., case distinction for l ≥ k vs. otherwise).

The boundedness guarantee continues to rely on assumptions not yet substantiated in the paper; empirical validation and safeguards (e.g., clipping) are planned but not shown, leaving the theoretical contribution modest.

Evaluation breadth remains limited in the current version: strong baselines (AdamW, spectral norm, Path-SGD/weight balancing, interval rescaling) and modern architectures (ResNet/ViT) are absent; VGG-16 results use only three seeds, and S&P is simplified, raising fairness concerns.
Clarity issues persist in the submission (pseudocode precision, formatting/typos, equation numbering, and explicit handling of layers without biases), which impedes reproducibility.
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors provide a thoughtful and technically detailed rebuttal outlining concrete plans to address the primary concerns: a formal block-wise proportionality result for BN/LN (including affine parameters and running statistics), a clarified algorithm with per-block scalers, a lemma and empirical diagnostics to justify the boundedness assumption, broader baselines and modern architectures, improved tuning fairness and seeds, schedule-robust variants, and significant clarity fixes. These changes would materially strengthen both soundness and evaluation. However, the current manuscript does not yet include these additions, and the central issues—rigor around normalization layers, reliance on unvalidated boundedness assumptions, limited baselines/architectures, and clarity/pseudocode ambiguities—remain. Given the strong emphasis on methodological soundness and convincing evaluation, I cannot recommend acceptance at this time. I encourage the authors to integrate the proposed theoretical extensions, empirical validations, expanded baselines, and modern architectures; with these enhancements, the work could become substantially more compelling for a future submission.

strengths: Practical, low-overhead method that directly targets a pervasive issue (plasticity loss due to unbounded weight growth) and integrates as a drop-in per-step procedure. Empirically, SWR improves VGG-16 on TinyImageNet from 39.1% ± 1.4% to 43.5% ± 0.3% without LR decay (Table 1) and to 52.2% ± 1.2% with LR decay (Table 5), indicating meaningful gains.

Clearer theoretical scope and rigor after rebuttal: proportionality is explicitly scoped to homogeneous activations; a block-wise proportionality treatment for BN/LN with affine and running-statistics handling is promised; boundedness assumptions are tied to bounded step sizes/gradients with empirical histograms planned; balancedness analysis remains applicable beyond homogeneity.

Expanded and more robust evaluation plan: adds stronger baselines (AdamW, spectral norm, weight clipping, weight balancing, interval rescaling), modern architectures (ResNet-18/34, Wide-ResNet-28-10, ViT/DeiT-Tiny), ≥5 seeds with confidence intervals, matched search budgets, and reports of wall-clock overhead.

Practical deployment improvements: introduces schedule-robust variants (LR-coupled λ_t and EMA target norms) and optimizer ablations (SGD, Adam, AdamW). Provides concrete default ranges for λ_f and λ_c across CNNs with/without BN and ViTs, plus promised sensitivity curves showing performance plateaus.

Reproducibility and clarity commitments: revised Algorithm 1 with explicit block-wise scaling rules for normalization layers and biases, a schematic for scaler flow, cleaned formatting, and code release with configs, seeds, and per-step scaler logs.
weaknesses: Novelty remains moderate relative to prior scaling/proportionality ideas; the contribution is primarily a well-executed, per-step, architecture-agnostic instantiation with practical gains and analysis.

Theory still relies on restrictive conditions for exact proportionality (homogeneous activations) and per-step bounded norm changes; effectiveness for non-homogeneous activations (e.g., GELU) is deferred to promised empirical coverage.

The two-coefficient setup (λ_f, λ_c) increases tuning complexity. While defaults are proposed, clear evidence of robustness across optimizers and schedulers should be included. Additionally, an explicit ablation on SWR frequency (per-step vs periodic) and on EMA-target variants would help quantify trade-offs.

Some results show L2 outperforming SWR in specific settings (e.g., CIFAR-100 with BN: 42.2% L2 vs 41.3% SWR in Table 1), underscoring the need for the planned broader baselines and sensitivity analyses to clarify when SWR is preferable.
reviewers.summary_comment: This work proposes Soft Weight Rescaling (SWR), a simple, per-step layerwise rescaling that curbs weight growth while attempting to preserve network behavior via a proportionality construction. It tackles a practically important problem and demonstrates clear benefits in warm-start and continual learning scenarios (Fig. 2–4), as well as in standard training, with notable improvements on VGG-16/TinyImageNet (e.g., 39.1% → 43.5% without LR decay, and 41.3% → 52.2% with step decay per Table 5). The rebuttal substantively strengthens the paper: it clarifies the theoretical scope, provides a more rigorous normalization-layer treatment, justifies boundedness with sufficient conditions and planned empirical validation, and commits to a significantly broader and fairer evaluation on modern architectures with stronger baselines, more seeds, and wall-clock reporting. It also adds schedule-robust SWR variants and concrete default λ ranges with sensitivity plots, directly addressing deployment concerns. While novelty is moderate and exact proportionality does not extend to non-homogeneous activations, the method’s simplicity, low overhead, and practical gains make it a useful contribution. Provided the promised additions are delivered in the camera-ready, I recommend acceptance.

strengths: Introduces a clear and original theoretical framework showing how chain-of-thought training can induce task decomposition in transformers, with rigorous proofs for both teacher-forced and end-to-end CoT settings.
Extends parity hardness results to a finite-sample, mean-squared-loss setting with approximate gradient oracles, strengthening the separation between direct training and CoT-based training.

Provides constructive, mechanistic gradient analyses explaining why attention weights concentrate on the correct intermediate nodes, yielding single-step learning with teacher forcing and logarithmic steps without it.
Demonstrates an elegant use of hierarchical causal masking and self-consistency via unlabeled data to control error propagation, aligning with empirical CoT practices.
Includes supporting numerical experiments that mirror the theoretical predictions and clearly demonstrate the success/failure modes across training regimes.
weaknesses: Positive results rely on a highly specialized architecture and training setup (position-only attention scores, a bespoke link function mapping averages to parity, weight quantization, and a designed filter for self-consistency), which may limit generality to realistic LLMs.

The no-teacher-forcing result assumes augmented unlabeled data and a level-aware causal mask that presumes knowledge of the decomposition tree, making the setting somewhat contrived and engineering-heavy.

The hardness lower bounds hinge on adversarial approximate gradient oracles and bounded-gradient conditions; while standard in theory, their practical relevance to standard training (with exact gradients) can be debated.
Some presentation artifacts/typos in the appendices and dense derivations slightly hinder readability and verification.
reviewers.summary_comment: This paper makes a strong, novel contribution by theoretically demonstrating how chain-of-thought supervision enables a one-layer transformer to solve the k-parity problem efficiently through explicit task decomposition. It extends known hardness results to finite samples and contrasts them with constructive positive results: one gradient step suffices with teacher forcing, and logarithmic steps suffice without teacher forcing when augmented with self-consistency checks. The mechanistic gradient analyses are insightful and align with CoT intuitions, and small-scale experiments substantiate the theory. While the setup is specialized (position-only attention, crafted link function, quantization, and a custom filter), the work provides valuable theoretical evidence that CoT-style training can induce stepwise reasoning in transformers. Given the originality and potential impact on understanding CoT training, acceptance is warranted despite the contrived assumptions and limited empirical scope.

why_it_fits: Strong topic fit with ICML’s core areas in theory of machine learning and deep learning: the paper proves optimization hardness and positive learning guarantees for transformers, directly aligning with ICML’s interests in statistical learning theory, optimization, and deep learning architectures.

Contribution type matches ICML’s value on principled theory: finite-sample hardness extensions, mechanistic gradient analyses, and provable benefits of chain-of-thought (CoT) training (teacher forcing vs. end-to-end with self-consistency). Reviewer notes emphasize rigorous proofs and insightful gradient analyses, consistent with ICML’s theoretical standards.

Novelty and significance judged positively by the reviewer (accept recommendation; high marks on novelty, soundness, significance). Establishing a separation between direct training and CoT-based training and showing single-step/logarithmic-step learning are timely theoretical contributions.

Rigor is high: the paper provides detailed proofs and controlled experiments that mirror the theoretical predictions, which aligns with ICML’s tolerance for theory-heavy work with light but illustrative experiments.

Presentation is mostly clear per the reviewer (clarity strong), and the framing around CoT reasoning, teacher forcing, and self-consistency connects to broad ML topics such as trustworthy ML and reasoning.
why_it_does_not_fit: Specialized and engineered setup (position-only attention scores, bespoke link function that maps averages to parity, quantization, and a custom filter) may limit generality and could raise concerns among ICML reviewers seeking broader applicability, as highlighted by the reviewer.
The no-teacher-forcing result presumes augmented unlabeled data and knowledge of the decomposition tree (level-aware masking), which may be seen as contrived relative to realistic LLM training.

Hardness results rely on adversarial approximate gradient oracles and bounded-gradient conditions; while standard in theory, their practical relevance can be questioned. Reviewer also rated evaluation lower, reflecting limited empirical breadth.

why_it_fits: Topical relevance to NeurIPS tracks in deep learning, theory, and decision-making: the work advances understanding of CoT reasoning in transformers, a hot NeurIPS theme linking theory with emerging practices in LLMs.

Novelty level is appropriate for NeurIPS: first theoretical analysis of training transformers to recursively generate intermediate states for parity, with clear contrasts between teacher-forced and end-to-end CoT.
why_it_does_not_fit: The setup is highly stylized (engineered link function, position-only attention, quantization, and custom self-consistency filter). NeurIPS’s broader, more applied audience may push for greater realism and broader empirical demonstrations; reviewer noted limited generality and evaluation.

why_it_fits: Directly about transformers and chain-of-thought reasoning, which are central to ICLR’s deep learning focus. The paper analyzes training dynamics and shows how CoT-style training induces stepwise reasoning, resonating with ICLR interest in representation and learning dynamics.

reviewers.summary_comment: The paper offers a novel and compelling theory of how transformers acquire stepwise reasoning when trained to generate chain-of-thought, with sharp separations between end-to-end learning and process-supervised training on k-parity. The authors’ rebuttal substantively addresses prior concerns by formalizing the link-function family and its realizations, establishing robustness to learned K/Q/V around positional initialization, adapting proofs to standard left-to-right masking (with O(k) steps), and extending the lower bound to stochastic-gradient oracles. They also commit to expanded experiments at larger scales with key ablations and visualizations. While the setting remains stylized and centered on parity, the core ideas are sound, significant, and clearly presented. I recommend acceptance, with the expectation that the promised theoretical clarifications and ablations are incorporated to solidify the evaluation and broaden applicability.

strengths: Substantive theoretical results remain rigorous and well supported: Theorem 2 extends parity hardness to the finite-sample empirical setting with approximate gradient oracles; Theorem 5 shows one-step learning with teacher forcing under objective (2) via a dominant O(d^{-2}) gradient signal; Theorem 7 establishes logarithmic-step convergence without teacher forcing under objective (4) using block autoregression and self-consistency, with gradient bounds provided by Lemma 4 and Lemma 6 and the causal mask in Figure 3.

Mechanistic clarity is strong: the gradient expansion at initialization (Eq. (6)) isolates leading terms that focus attention on the correct child indices; higher-order interaction terms are tightly controlled by concentration (Appendix B), explaining why attention aligns with the hierarchical decomposition and how error propagation is curtailed by the mask.

The rebuttal adds robustness and practicality: a planned Proposition B.1 to handle learned K/Q/V near positional initialization, a general φ-family (Assumption A.1) with explicit shallow-MLP realizations, and a Remark 2.1 adapting the proof to standard left-to-right masking (with O(k) stages). They also propose a smooth gating version of the self-consistency filter and Corollary A.1 linking the oracle model to unbiased stochastic gradients, improving the connection to typical training.

Evaluation will be substantially strengthened: the authors commit to expanded experiments at larger d (128, 256), multi-head variants, learned K/Q/V, learned φ, mask ablations (block vs. standard), minibatch SGD and injected noise, with/without rounding, and sensitivity sweeps over ε0 and n′, alongside visualizations of attention focusing. This directly addresses earlier concerns about empirical scope.

Clarity improvements are promised and targeted: correcting notation glitches (e.g., indices d_ℓ, h[m]), restating that the block mask is target-agnostic, and moving key proof intuition (trivial vs. nontrivial interaction terms) into the main text, which should further improve accessibility.
weaknesses: Despite robustness sketches and planned ablations, the core proofs still rely on stylized assumptions (positional-only attention at or near initialization, flat-derivative φ around 0 and ±1, and an architecture-defined block mask). While reasonable for analysis, the generality to more standard LLM configurations remains partially theoretical until the expanded experiments are incorporated.

The no-teacher-forcing result depends on a bespoke self-consistency mechanism (now a smooth gate) and augmented unlabeled data; practical sensitivity to ε0 and n′ requires the promised empirical validation. Theoretical guarantees still hinge on thresholding behavior that must be tuned in practice.
Scope remains focused on parity, which limits broader significance. Extensions or comparisons to other Boolean functions or structured reasoning tasks are not yet explored.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper provides a careful theoretical account of how chain-of-thought (CoT) supervision enables transformers to solve k-parity efficiently. Theorem 2 proves a finite-sample hardness result for end-to-end training with approximate gradient oracles, complementing prior population-level results. In contrast, Theorem 5 shows that with teacher forcing (objective (2)), a one-layer transformer learns the hierarchical 2-parity decomposition in one update by concentrating attention on the correct child indices; the core mechanism is made explicit via the gradient expansion (Eq. (6)) and concentration arguments in Appendix B. Theorem 7 further shows that without teacher forcing (objective (4)), a block autoregressive mask (Figure 3) plus self-consistency via augmented unlabeled data yields logarithmic-step convergence, with gradient propagation bounded by Lemma 6. Experiments (Figures 4–5) qualitatively corroborate the theory (failure without CoT, staged convergence with teacher forcing or self-consistency). My initial concerns about stylized modeling choices, bespoke filtering, limited experiments, and stringent asymptotic assumptions are largely addressed in the rebuttal: the authors commit to Proposition B.1 (learned K/Q/V), Assumption A.1 (general φ realized by small MLPs), Remark 2.1 (standard masking), a smooth gate with ε0 and n′ sweeps, and Corollary A.1 (stochastic gradients), alongside comprehensive ablations and scaling. Assuming these additions are integrated, the evaluation and clarity are compelling, and the theoretical insights are sound and valuable. I recommend acceptance.

strengths: Clear theoretical separations and mechanisms: finite-sample hardness without intermediate supervision (Theorem 2), one-step learning with teacher forcing using the CoT loss over intermediate states (Eq. (2), Theorem 5), and logarithmic-step convergence without teacher forcing via self-consistency and a hierarchical mask (Eq. (3), Eq. (4), Theorem 7).

Mechanistic gradient analysis that isolates dominant O(d^{-2}) signals on true child indices and controls higher-order terms; bounded gradients are established (Lemma 4, Lemma 6), and the proofs carefully handle concentration of nontrivial interactions and error propagation across CoT steps.

Rebuttal substantially strengthens practical relevance: commitments to ablations with fully learned K/Q/V, learned small MLPs for φ (with Assumption A.1 and explicit two-layer ReQU parameters), multi-head variants, and mask comparisons; replacement of hard zeroing with smooth gating (sigmoid) based on confidence proxies; sensitivity sweeps over gating thresholds and augmentation size; and inclusion of larger-scale experiments and baselines (RNN/GRU, deeper transformers).

Empirical evidence aligns with theory: the direct model plateaus at ~0.5 prediction loss, while CoT with teacher forcing and self-consistency show staged improvements; Figure 4 and Appendix D illustrate phase transitions that correspond to level-by-level solving, with dashed lines marking filter deactivations in the self-consistency variant.

Improved clarity and reproducibility plans: explicit MLP parameters for φ, a robustness proposition (Proposition B.1) for small content components in K/Q/V around positional initialization, clearer notation and architectural independence of masks, attention heatmaps, and code release.
weaknesses: The model remains stylized (position-driven attention at initialization, specially constructed φ family, hierarchical block mask). While the rebuttal argues robustness and adds ablations, transfer to standard LLMs and broader tasks is still uncertain and largely contingent on promised additions.

The no-teacher-forcing approach depends on augmentation and gating; although the authors propose smooth gating and data-driven thresholds (τℓ) with sensitivity sweeps, the practical overheads and robustness across settings need to be demonstrated, and the scope beyond parity remains discussed but unvalidated.

Current experiments are limited (e.g., d = 64, k ≤ 32, single architecture). The enhanced empirical program (larger d, multi-heads, learned K/Q/V and φ, SGD noise, broader baselines) must be incorporated to substantiate generality and practical impact.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper provides a rigorous and insightful account of how chain-of-thought training enables task decomposition and efficient learning for transformers on k-parity. It extends hardness to finite samples (Theorem 2) and shows constructive optimization guarantees with process supervision (Theorem 5) and with self-consistency and a hierarchical mask (Theorem 7). The gradient-based analysis explains why attention concentrates on the correct child indices, and the experiments (Figure 4, Appendix D) qualitatively corroborate staged learning. The rebuttal directly addresses practical concerns by committing to robustness analyses (Proposition B.1 for K/Q/V around positional initialization), learned φ via shallow MLPs, multi-head ablations, mask comparisons, smooth gating with sensitivity sweeps, larger-scale runs, and broader baselines; clarity and reproducibility will also be improved with explicit parameters, visualizations, and code. While the setup remains stylized and broader applicability beyond parity is still to be empirically demonstrated, the theoretical contributions are strong, the practical alignment has improved, and the planned additions should meaningfully bolster evaluation. I recommend acceptance.

strengths: Introduces a creative and conceptually compelling idea: computing data-dependent prototypes via backward integration of an explicit Flow Matching velocity and training a one-step generator that eliminates ODE solving at inference.
Grounds the coupling in an explicit FM velocity derived from prior theory, offering a principled way to reduce training variance and obtain straighter trajectories for pairing.
Extensible framework: supports conditional generation with labels and demonstrates applicability beyond images (e.g., color transfer in RGB space), suggesting broad potential.
Clear articulation of the practical role of the regularization parameter sigma to ensure invertibility and address manifold-support issues, with illustrative 2D experiments.
weaknesses: Empirical evaluation is very limited: primarily 2D toy data and MNIST, with no quantitative metrics (e.g., FID/IS), no comparisons to strong baselines (FM/OT-CFM/Rectified Flow/one-step distillation), and no ablations on key components (buffer size, sigma, solver tol, discretization).

Core methodological assumptions are not rigorously validated: the importance-sampling discretization may be biased, the claim that prototypes follow the prior distribution is unproven under approximations, and the near-monotonic coupling property is asserted without formal or empirical verification.

The approach incurs substantial training-time cost due to solving inverse ODEs for prototypes and may not scale to high-dimensional data; practical speed–quality trade-offs and wall-clock comparisons are missing.

Clarity and presentation issues (notation inconsistencies, reference formatting, limited theoretical justification) make it difficult to assess generality and limits; use of a heavy DiT backbone for MNIST further obscures scalability.
reviewers.summary_comment: This paper proposes an ambitious and novel direction: leveraging an explicit Flow Matching velocity to compute approximate prototypes by integrating backward from target samples, then training a one-step generator that bypasses ODE solvers at inference. The idea is intriguing, potentially impactful for fast generation, and the use of a reversible conditional map with small sigma to address invertibility is thoughtful. However, the current evidence base is too thin to substantiate the main claims. Experiments are limited to toy 2D and MNIST without quantitative metrics or comparisons, and key claims (quality preservation, monotonic couplings, prototype distribution matching the prior) remain largely unverified under the proposed approximations. The training-time burden and scalability concerns are not addressed. While the concept is promising and worthy of further exploration, the paper in its present form does not meet the bar for acceptance. Strengthening the empirical evaluation (standard datasets, quantitative metrics, ablations, and runtime analysis) and providing more rigorous support for the coupling/prototype properties would significantly improve the work.

reviewers.summary_comment: This work introduces a compelling direction for one-step generative modeling: use the explicit flow-matching velocity (Eq. (1)) to construct per-sample prototypes by solving the inverse ODE (Eq. (2)), then train a deterministic one-step map (Algorithms 1/2). The rebuttal substantively improves the theoretical grounding—clarifying the role of σ>0 (Sec. 3.3), interpreting vσ as a conditional-mean flow, providing a stability bound via Grönwall’s inequality, and fixing the SNIS discretization—and outlines a rigorous experimental plan with appropriate baselines, metrics, ablations, and speed analyses, plus scalability strategies and code release. However, the current submission still lacks these quantitative results and comparisons; Figures 1–4 remain qualitative, and there is no reporting of prototype NFEs, ESS behavior, or speed/quality trade-offs. Given my emphasis on novelty and impact, I find the idea exciting and the planned additions promising, but the present empirical evaluation is insufficient to support acceptance. I encourage the authors to incorporate the proposed experiments—e.g., CIFAR-10 and CelebA with Rectified Flow/OT-CFM/distillation baselines, FID/Precision–Recall with confidence intervals, inference latency vs. NFE, ablations over N/σ/tolerances, ESS diagnostics—and to resolve the reference and clarity issues by explicitly anchoring claims to Eq. (1), Eq. (2), and Algorithms 1/2. With these in place, the paper could make a strong and impactful contribution.

strengths: The authors correct the discretization in Eq. (1) by including the missing squared denominator in the softmax exponent and propose a principled temperature control (τ) to maintain reasonable ESS; they commit to re-running experiments with the corrected weights and reporting ESS diagnostics.

Methodological framing is strengthened: they plan to formalize invertibility for σ > 0 (Sec. 3.3) via Jacobian analysis, provide Grönwall-based stability bounds for prototype errors when using approximate velocities in Eq. (2), and connect function-approximation error to a W2 pushforward bound; they also propose two-sample tests (MMD/energy distance) to assess whether prototypes match ρ0.

A comprehensive evaluation plan is outlined: quantitative metrics (FID/KID/IS/Precision-Recall) on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CelebA; comparisons to Rectified Flow, OT-CFM, and distilled one-step baselines; detailed runtime and amortization analyses; and extensive ablations over σ, ODE tolerances, buffer size N, ESS/temperature τ, and SNIS versus rejection sampling.

They propose concrete engineering mitigations for high-dimensional SNIS (PCA/whitening, k-NN gating, a bridging schedule in t) and promise clarity improvements (theorem/lemma boxes, reorganized methodology from Eq. (1) to SNIS discretization, Algorithm details, and code release).
weaknesses: The manuscript still lacks quantitative results, updated figures with the corrected discretization, baseline comparisons, and runtime analyses; thus, claims about performance, stability, and speed are not yet substantiated.

The proposed mitigations (temperature τ, k-NN gating, whitening, bridging schedule) introduce additional hyperparameters and complexity; their efficacy and robustness remain untested, and the risk of low ESS in high dimensions persists without empirical validation.

The theoretical guarantees depend on assumptions (uniform approximation error, bounded domains, Lipschitz constants) that are not verified in practice; the pushforward bound requires sup-norm control that is unlikely to be certified empirically, leaving a gap between theory and implementation.

Training cost remains potentially high due to inverse ODE solves per sample and buffer-based weighting, but no concrete NFE, wall-clock, or memory figures are provided in the current submission to assess trade-offs.
reviewers.summary_comment: The rebuttal substantially improves the methodological clarity and proposes strong additions: a corrected SNIS discretization for Eq. (1), stability analyses for the inverse ODE in Eq. (2), distributional pushforward bounds, ESS-aware weighting with temperature, and a thorough evaluation plan including baselines and runtime/amortization studies. These revisions would address the core concerns about soundness and evaluation. However, the current manuscript still presents no quantitative results with the corrected formulation, no comparisons to established baselines, and no runtime evidence. Given the emphasis on rigorous validation, I must recommend rejection at this time. I encourage the authors to incorporate the promised analyses and experiments—especially ESS diagnostics, ablations, baseline quality–speed trade-offs, and updated results with the corrected weights—and resubmit; the approach remains promising if empirically validated.

strengths: Appealing, practical goal: construct source–target pairs via the explicit flow-matching velocity (Eq. (1)) by integrating the inverse ODE (Eq. (2)) and train a direct one-step map with losses (Eq. (3)/(4)), yielding ODE-free inference; Algorithms 1/2 are clearly stated.

reviewers.summary_comment: The paper proposes an intuitive and practical pathway to one-step sampling for flow matching: compute approximate prototypes via the explicit velocity (Eq. (1)) by solving the inverse ODE (Eq. (2)), then train a direct map (Eqs. (3)/(4)) to eliminate ODE solves at inference. This direction is appealing, and the training/inference pipelines (Algorithms 1/2) are clearly described. The authors’ rebuttal constructively addresses key points: they clarify the role of σ for invertibility, correct a scaling mistake in the discretized importance weights, outline ESS-based monitoring, provide a stability argument via Grönwall, and commit to thorough quantitative benchmarks and runtime studies with strong baselines and practical accelerations. However, these crucial elements are not yet reflected in the submission, and the acknowledged discretization error calls into question the current qualitative results. strengths: Introduces a general theoretical framework for analog training under generic asymmetric, non-linear response functions, moving beyond prior work restricted to special linear cases.

Reveals an implicit penalization effect induced by asymmetric device responses and formalizes it via a new objective f(W) + <Σ, R_c(W)>, offering a clear and actionable understanding of why Analog SGD exhibits asymptotic bias.

Proposes a principled Residual Learning (bilevel) approach that aligns algorithmic stationary points with hardware symmetric points and proves exact convergence under zero-shifted symmetry, explaining and generalizing Tiki-Taka with non-zero mixing.

Provides extensions (Residual Learning v2) that address two critical non-idealities—IO noise and limited update granularity—via a digital buffer and thresholded transfer, with empirical gains on ResNet/MobileNet benchmarks.

Backs theory with comprehensive simulations using AIHWKIT across MNIST and CIFAR10/100 with multiple architectures and response families (power/exponential), including ablations on the mixing coefficient γ.
weaknesses: The main convergence guarantee for Residual Learning relies on strong convexity and zero-shifted symmetric points; practical deep networks are non-convex, and achieving/maintaining G(0)=0 universally may be nontrivial in hardware.

All empirical evidence is simulator-based with no on-hardware validation, and robustness to device-to-device/cycle variations and broader analog non-idealities is not fully characterized despite being discussed conceptually.

The approximate analog update model depends on small response granularity and bounded response functions; the practical range of validity and sensitivity to real device drift or calibration errors could be elaborated further.
reviewers.summary_comment: This work delivers a strong, novel, and impactful theoretical foundation for analog in-memory training under generic non-ideal response functions. It identifies and formalizes an implicit penalty induced by asymmetric responses, proving that Analog SGD converges inexactly with a non-vanishing asymptotic error, and introduces a Residual Learning framework—interpreted as a bilevel optimization—that provably attains exact convergence when the hardware response is zero-shifted around the origin. The theory explains the limitations of prior Tiki-Taka results in non-ideal settings and justifies the benefit of non-zero mixing. Beyond theory, the paper extends the method to address IO noise and update granularity and validates claims with extensive AIHWKIT simulations on MNIST and CIFAR10/100 with modern architectures. While some assumptions (strong convexity, zero-shift feasibility) and the lack of hardware experiments limit the scope, the core ideas are creative, forward-looking, and likely to influence both algorithm and device co-design. Given the strong novelty and significance with acceptable soundness and solid empirical support, I recommend acceptance.

strengths: The authors address residual concerns with concrete additions: a formal RLv2 proposition with explicit assumptions (q_min/q_max bounds, Lipschitz F,G near 0, M_min^RL>0, martingale-difference I/O noise) and step-size policies (α ≤ 1/(4 L F_max), β = c1 α γ^{3/2} with β capped by c2/√K and a BL-aware rule), yielding E_K^{RLv2} ≤ O(√(σ^2/K)) + O(Σ_ε^2 + Δw_min^2 + ε^2).

They provide a clear robustness path for imperfect zero-shift by analyzing |G(0)| ≤ ε and showing an ε^2 asymptotic dependence via S_K^{RL} ≤ L_S^2 E||P_k||^2 + ε^2/F_min, plus a practical calibration-and-monitoring recipe and open-sourcing scripts to map measured q_± to F,G and select references.

The approximation regime for pulse-to-continuous dynamics is quantified using the decomposition from Eq. (31), and they will empirically sweep (α, Δw_min) and report realized BL and response factors, improving trust in the analog update model.

They commit to stress tests in low-BL regimes (BL ∈ {1,2,4}) with BL-aware β capping and stochastic thresholding and to an additional end-to-end analog CIFAR-10 ConvNet experiment, broadening empirical validation beyond last-layer fine-tuning.

The core theoretical contributions remain strong and novel: the F/G decomposition revealing implicit penalties from asymmetry, inexact convergence of Analog SGD for generic responses, and a principled bilevel Residual Learning framework with exact convergence under zero-shift, which also explains and strengthens Tiki-Taka.
weaknesses: On-chip validation remains pending; several strengthened pieces (formal RLv2 guarantees, misalignment experiments, low-BL stress tests, end-to-end CIFAR-10 ConvNet) are promised for the camera-ready and not yet present, leaving some practical claims provisional.

Although BL-aware control and stochastic thresholding are proposed, robustness to very coarse granularity, larger misalignment, and device-to-device variations across hardware remains to be demonstrated experimentally; reliance on careful calibration persists.
reviewers.summary_comment: The second rebuttal further strengthens the paper with precise assumptions, step-size and BL-aware policies, and asymptotic error characterizations for RLv2 under noisy I/O, quantization, and small misalignment (O(Σ_ε^2 + Δw_min^2 + ε^2)). The misalignment analysis (|G(0)| ≤ ε with ε^2 dependence) and a concrete zero-shift calibration-and-monitoring protocol, along with open-sourced scripts, address practical feasibility. The authors also quantify the pulse-to-continuous approximation using the Eq. (31) scaling and will corroborate it with parameter sweeps and reporting of realized BL. Planned low-BL stress tests and an end-to-end analog CIFAR-10 ConvNet experiment expand empirical scope. While on-chip validation and some additions remain promised for the camera-ready, the responses convincingly mitigate earlier concerns and enhance confidence in both the theoretical soundness and practical relevance. Given the high novelty and potential impact of the framework, I maintain my recommendation to accept.

strengths: The authors address remaining theoretical concerns by replacing global strong convexity with PL or local strong-convexity in the RL analysis (affecting the contraction step around inequality (121)) and by noting a subgradient-based extension for piecewise-Lipschitz response functions; this strengthens the applicability of Theorems 1–3 beyond the original assumptions.

They promote a formal convergence proposition for RLv2 with explicit assumptions and stepsizes, yielding E_K^{RLv2} ≤ O(√(σ^2/K)) + O(Σ_ε^2 + Δw_min^2 + ε^2), thereby aligning the practical noisy/quantized variant with the main theoretical guarantees.

The approximation regime of Theorem 4 will be quantified via sweeps over (α, Δw_min, σ_c), reporting realized bit lengths (BL) and normalized errors, and tied to performance; this directly addresses the small-update approximation and shows when BL is sufficiently large (e.g., Δw_min≈1e−3, BL cap=32 in Table 4).

Evaluation will broaden with a fully analog end-to-end CIFAR-10 ConvNet and additional baselines (mixed-precision update and ASGD+momentum), in addition to existing MNIST/CIFAR fine-tuning where RL/TT markedly improve over ASGD (e.g., ResNet18 on CIFAR100: ASGD 68.98±1.01 vs RL 76.17±0.23, TTv2 78.56±0.29, RLv2 79.83±0.13, DSGD 81.12±0.25).

Practical guidance is strengthened: a zero-shift calibration protocol (per [39]) with online correction, robustness characterization for small misalignment |G(0)|≤ε, an adaptive γ-selection heuristic, and BL-aware β capping, plus release of scripts to map measured q_± to F,G and calibrate the reference.
weaknesses: Hardware validation remains pending; although a chip-in-the-loop plan and calibration scripts are promised, current evidence is still simulator-based, and robustness to larger miscalibration and device variability across arrays has yet to be empirically demonstrated.

Several key additions (PL/local-strong-convexity revision, formal RLv2 proposition and proof, expanded experiments and baselines) are commitments for the camera-ready; until incorporated, guarantees for RLv2 and end-to-end applicability to larger nonconvex models remain partly inferential.

The method relies on maintaining conditions such as M_min^RL>0 (avoiding saturation) and effective zero-shift; while the authors propose calibration and BL-aware controls, the operational envelope and failure modes under heavy saturation or coarse granularity still need comprehensive empirical mapping.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper offers a rigorous and practically motivated analysis of analog in-memory training under generic asymmetric response functions: it identifies an implicit penalty (Theorem 1), proves inexact convergence of Analog SGD (Theorem 2), and proposes a bilevel residual learning framework with convergence guarantees and exact convergence under zero-shift (Theorem 3, Corollary 1). The authors’ second response further strengthens the work by (i) relaxing strong convexity to PL/local conditions and extending to piecewise-smooth responses, (ii) formalizing RLv2 with an additive asymptotic term O(Σ_ε^2 + Δw_min^2 + ε^2), (iii) quantifying the small-step approximation regime with (α, Δw_min, σ_c) sweeps and BL reporting, and (iv) broadening evaluation with fully analog end-to-end CIFAR-10 and added baselines. Existing results already show sizable gains of RL/TT over ASGD (e.g., ResNet18 on CIFAR100: ~+7–11% absolute), and the proposed additions address robustness to misalignment, device variability, and practical hyperparameter selection (γ, β). Although on-hardware results are not yet included and some contributions are pledged for the camera-ready, the core methodology and analysis are sound and impactful, and the planned extensions make the evaluation sufficiently compelling. I recommend acceptance.

strengths: The rebuttal substantively addresses key theory concerns: replacing global strong convexity with PL/local-strong-convexity for the lower-level problem, adding robustness to imperfect zero-shift with an explicit additive O(σ^2 ε^2) term, and formalizing RLv2 with convergence bounds that include O(Σ_ε^2 + Δw_min^2 + ε^2) under BL-aware step-size conditions.

The authors provide a practical pathway to deployment: a zero-shift calibration protocol, an online adaptation heuristic, and a planned measurement-to-model toolkit that maps measured q_± to F,G, selects a reference array to minimize |G(0)|, and reports BL/transfer statistics.

Evaluation breadth is improved: a fully analog end-to-end CIFAR-10 ConvNet, broader baselines (mixed-precision update and ASGD+momentum), and systematic regime sweeps over α, Δw_min, and σ_c, plus device-shaped responses fitted to PCM/ReRAM/ECRAM curves and stress tests with per-element variability and miscalibration.

They will quantify the pulse-to-dynamics approximation (Theorem 4/Eq. 31) with measured normalized errors and BL distributions, and provide regime maps highlighting when RLv2 outperforms TTv2 (e.g., stronger asymmetry/misalignment, tighter granularity, higher noise), along with overhead metrics (pulse counts, transfer triggers).

The planned additions enhance clarity and reproducibility: explicit assumptions for RLv2, BL-aware β capping, thresholding/dithering details, and scripts to support calibration and monitoring in practice.
weaknesses: On-silicon validation remains uncertain; chip-in-the-loop results are planned but not guaranteed, so robustness to real device non-idealities is still primarily supported by simulator studies and fitted response curves.

Several key improvements (relaxed assumptions, robustness bounds, RLv2 proposition, expanded experiments) are promised for the camera-ready; their absence in the current manuscript makes some conclusions contingent on follow-through.

End-to-end analog demonstrations remain on relatively small models; scalability and stability for deeper networks under strong asymmetry and coarse granularity still require further empirical evidence.

Even with the planned regime sweeps, consistent superiority of RLv2 over TTv2 across diverse settings must be clearly established; in some reported cases the gains are modest, and broader, systematic comparisons will be important.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper tackles a practically important gap in analog in-memory training by modeling generic asymmetric/non-linear response functions, showing the resulting implicit penalty and inexact convergence of Analog SGD, and proposing a Residual Learning framework (and RLv2) with convergence guarantees. strengths: Introduces a clear and compelling dynamical picture of diffusion-model training with two distinct timescales: an early generalization time τgen that is independent of dataset size and a later memorization time τmem that grows linearly with n, yielding a widening early-stopping window.

Provides a principled theoretical explanation using a tractable random-features model and random matrix theory, deriving a two-bulk spectrum for the training dynamics that maps directly to the observed timescales and their scaling laws.

Presents convincing empirical evidence with U-Nets on CelebA, including scaling collapses (τgen ∝ 1/W and τmem ∝ n/W), tracking both FID and an explicit memorization fraction, and controls (e.g., full-batch) that rule out sample-repetition artifacts.
Bridges theory and practice effectively: fixed-t analytical results connect to real training behavior, and the (n,p,τ) phase diagram provides actionable guidance on early stopping and model capacity.
Good reproducibility practices (released code, statistical confidence intervals, and ablations) enhance credibility and utility.
weaknesses: Empirical validation is confined to a single grayscale 32×32 CelebA setting and unconditional models; broader tests on higher-resolution, diverse datasets and modalities would strengthen generality claims.

The mechanistic link to spectral bias and low-noise irregularity is compelling but remains partly qualitative in neural networks; the theoretical analysis assumes random features, fixed t, and gradient flow with infinitesimal step size, which may deviate from practical U-Net training across times with optimizers like Adam.

Memorization is assessed via nearest-neighbor ratios and FID at low resolution; while standard, these proxies may not capture all forms of memorization or originality and could be sensitive to dataset specifics.
reviewers.summary_comment: This paper advances a timely and important question: why and when do diffusion models avoid memorization despite overparameterization? The authors identify two training timescales—τgen and τmem—with compelling empirical scaling laws (τgen roughly independent of n and τmem ∝ n, both modulated by capacity), and substantiate these findings with a rigorous random-features analysis that reveals a two-bulk spectral structure controlling learning dynamics. The resulting picture of implicit dynamical regularization and the (n,p,τ) phase diagram are novel and potentially influential for practice (early stopping, capacity control). While experiments are limited in scope and the mechanistic interpretation in deep nets is partly qualitative, the combination of empirical scaling collapses, theoretical derivation, and practical implications is strong. Given the novelty and potential impact, I recommend acceptance.

strengths: Presents a clear and original two-timescale training picture (τgen for achieving high-quality generation and τmem for the onset of memorization) with compelling empirical scaling laws (τmem ∝ n; τgen, τmem ∝ W−1) and practical early-stopping guidance.

Develops a principled theoretical explanation via random features and random matrix theory: a two-bulk spectral separation of the feature correlation matrix accounts for the emergence of distinct timescales and their dependence on n and p.

Careful experimental methodology (including full-batch controls) and convincing curve collapses demonstrate that delayed memorization is not due to sample repetition but to dynamics tied to dataset size and capacity.

Rebuttal substantially strengthens the contribution with concrete commitments: broader datasets/resolutions (color CelebA 32×32/64×64, CIFAR-10 32×32), architectural ablations (EDM/VDM-style backbones), complementary metrics (KID, precision/recall, LPIPS/CLIP-based memorization), and reporting of scaling fit uncertainties.

Improved theory–practice linkage in the rebuttal: a quantitative rationale for low-t dominance, an integral-over-t operator perspective, and a planned t-wise loss analysis showing earliest gaps at small t while preserving n- and W-scaling; plus enhanced reproducibility with added scripts and environment specs.
weaknesses: As submitted, empirical validation remains confined to 32×32 grayscale CelebA (and synthetic data); broader datasets, higher resolutions, and alternative architectures are promised but not yet included.

The mapping from fixed-t RFNN under Gaussian/high-dimensional assumptions to time-conditioned U-Nets remains partly heuristic until the proposed t-wise analyses and operator integration are incorporated; more quantitative sensitivity across t in the main paper would strengthen the claim.

Memorization and quality assessments primarily use L2 nearest-neighbor and FID in the submission; while the authors commit to LPIPS/CLIP and KID/precision–recall and to reporting confidence intervals for scaling fits, these additions are not yet visible.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper offers a novel and compelling account of why overparameterized diffusion models often avoid memorization, identifying two distinct training timescales and showing that the memorization time grows with dataset size while the generalization time stays essentially fixed. The theory, grounded in random features and random matrix analysis, connects these timescales to a two-bulk spectral separation and aligns well with the empirical scaling behavior. The rebuttal meaningfully addresses prior concerns by committing to broader datasets/resolutions, architectural ablations, richer memorization/quality metrics, explicit uncertainty reporting for scaling fits, and a clearer quantitative link from fixed-t theory to time-conditioned practice via low-t dominance and operator integration. While these additions are not yet reflected in the submission, the core idea is original, insightful, and practically relevant, with acceptable methodological rigor. I recommend acceptance.

strengths: Clear and well-supported identification of two distinct training timescales (τgen and τmem) with convincing scaling collapses (τmem ∝ n and both τgen, τmem ∝ W−1), and controls (e.g., full-batch updates) that rule out trivial sample-repetition effects.

Principled random-features analysis that links the dynamics to a two-bulk spectral structure of the training operator, explaining the emergence of τgen (population-structure, Σ-dependent) versus τmem (dataset-dependent) and the scaling τmem ∝ n in the overparameterized regime.

Rebuttal significantly strengthens evaluation by committing to diversified memorization and quality metrics (LPIPS/CLIP for NN retrieval, KID, precision/recall, density/coverage), explicit operational definitions of τgen and τmem, and reporting slope estimates with confidence intervals and sensitivity analyses.

Rebuttal improves theory–practice alignment: clarifies dependence on general covariance Σ, addresses time-conditioning via an integrated operator and low-t dominance, and provides an alternative route to the spectrum beyond replica arguments (GEP/linear-pencil sketch).

Expanded empirical scope promised in the revision (CIFAR-10 32×32, color CelebA 64×64, and an EDM/VDM-style backbone with attention) and normalization by ψn = n/d to test cross-resolution predictions enhance external validity.
weaknesses: Several key improvements (additional datasets/resolutions/architectures, diversified metrics, t-wise analyses, and uncertainty-quantified scaling fits) are commitments for the camera-ready; the current submission would benefit from including at least a subset to fully validate robustness.

Despite mitigation via whitening and synthetic checks, the theoretical framework still relies on random features with sub-Gaussian assumptions and a fixed first layer; the mapping to full U-Nets on potentially heavy-tailed, structured data remains partially heuristic.

Empirical scale remains modest relative to state-of-the-art regimes; larger models/resolutions and more diverse architectures would further substantiate generality and stress-test the phase boundaries.
reviewers.summary_comment: This paper provides a coherent dynamical explanation for why diffusion models often avoid memorization: training exhibits two distinct timescales, with an early generalization time τgen and a later memorization time τmem that scales linearly with dataset size, creating a widening early-stopping window. The empirical results show clear scaling collapses and careful controls, while the random-features analysis yields a tractable spectral mechanism with two bulks corresponding to generalization and memorization dynamics. The rebuttal addresses prior concerns with concrete, rigorous additions: diversified memorization and quality metrics, explicit operational definitions and uncertainty-quantified scaling fits, systematic coverage across diffusion times (including time-weighted losses), and broader empirical scope (additional datasets, higher resolution, and an architectural ablation). Some limitations persist—scale and architectural breadth, and a remaining theory–practice gap—but the methodology is sound, the evaluation (including planned additions) is strong, and the contribution is significant. I recommend acceptance.

strengths: The paper identifies two distinct training timescales—τgen for achieving good sample quality and τmem for the onset of memorization—and demonstrates clear empirical scaling laws (τmem ∝ n and τgen, τmem ∝ W−1), yielding a practical early-stopping window that grows with n.

Empirical rigor and theoretical grounding: careful experiments with curve collapses and full-batch controls are complemented by a tractable random-features analysis explaining the timescales via distinct spectral bulks of the feature correlation operator.

The rebuttal substantially enhances practical relevance with concrete additions: broader datasets and resolutions (CIFAR-10, 64×64 CelebA), architectural ablations (EDM/VDM-style backbones), richer memorization and quality metrics (LPIPS/CLIP nearest neighbors, KID, precision/recall, density/coverage), and systematic t-wise loss analysis supporting low-t dominance.

Clear, reproducible presentation with stated assumptions, error bars, code release, and in the rebuttal, explicit operational definitions for τgen and τmem and plans to report slope estimates and confidence intervals for scaling laws.
Actionable training guidance: early-stopping criteria focused on low-t train/test gaps, capacity control via τ scaling with W and n, and diagnostic scripts planned for the repository.
weaknesses: As submitted, empirical validation remains focused on low-resolution CelebA and a single architecture family; while the authors commit to broader experiments and metrics, these results are not yet present in the main paper.

Memorization detection in the submission primarily relies on pixel-space L2 with a fixed threshold; the promised LPIPS/CLIP-based analyses and additional quality/diversity metrics will address this but are currently absent.

Theoretical mapping to time-conditioned U-Nets, optimizers, and full t-schedules remains idealized; even with the planned t-integration analysis and sensitivity studies, the connection to complex architectures will stay partly heuristic.
reviewers.summary_comment: This work addresses a practically important question: why diffusion models often avoid memorization during training. It presents a clear, empirically supported picture with two timescales—τgen (good sample quality) and τmem (memorization)—and shows that τmem scales linearly with dataset size while τgen remains roughly constant, creating an early-stopping window that widens with n. The experiments are methodically executed, and the random-features analysis provides a convincing mechanism via spectral separation of training dynamics. The authors’ rebuttal effectively strengthens the paper’s practical value with concrete commitments: adding broader datasets/resolutions and backbones, robust memorization metrics (LPIPS/CLIP), additional quality/diversity metrics, systematic t-wise analysis and operational definitions for τgen/τmem with uncertainty estimates, plus practical training recipes. While the current submission’s empirical scope and memorization metric are still limited and the theory-to-practice mapping remains somewhat idealized, the core findings are significant, the presentation is clear and reproducible, and the planned additions directly address prior concerns. I recommend acceptance.
